title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: vignette demonstrates tinydenseR can applied publicly available clinical mass cytometry (CyTOF) data identify immune cell density changes associated response anti-PD-1 immunotherapy. tinydenseR can also used scRNA-seq data, natively supports cytometry inputs via flowCore::flowSet flowWorkspace::cytoset objects. vignette illustrates cytometry workflow end--end. use data Krieg et al. (2018), profiled peripheral blood mononuclear cells melanoma patients baseline (treatment) using high-dimensional mass cytometry. Patients subsequently classified responders (R) non-responders (NR) based clinical outcome anti-PD-1 immunotherapy. key finding original study frequency CD14+CD16−HLA-DRhi monocytes commencing therapy strong predictor progression-free overall survival.

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: core workflow follows four steps: RunTDR() — build landmarks flowSet batch correction compute sample-level density profiles. Visualize landmark sample embeddings — inspect UMAP landmarks sample PCA confirm biological structure batch correction. get.lm() — perform differential density analysis controlling batch effects. get.plsD() — decompose density contrast interpretable marker-driven components.

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: dataset available Bioconductor HDCytoData package Krieg_Anti_PD_1_flowSet(). original study described : Krieg C, Nowicka M, Guglietta S, Schindler S, Hartmann FJ, Weber LM, Dber R, Robinson MD, Pelber SJ, Christopoulos P, et al. High-dimensional single-cell analysis predicts response anti-PD-1 immunotherapy. Nature Medicine 24, 144–153 (2018).

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
code: library(tinydenseR) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork) library(flowCore) library(HDCytoData) library(SummarizedExperiment)

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: load Krieg et al. anti-PD-1 dataset directly HDCytoData Bioconductor package. returns flowSet containing one FCS file per sample, expression data embedded sample-level metadata columns (group_id, batch_id, sample_id).
code: Krieg_Anti_PD_1 <-   HDCytoData::Krieg_Anti_PD_1_flowSet()  # Extract marker channel information from the flowSet MARKER_INFO <-   Krieg_Anti_PD_1@frames$`BASE_CK_2016-06-23_03_NR1.fcs`@description$MARKER_INFO

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: HDCytoData package encodes group_id (response status), batch_id, sample_id columns expression matrix rather flowSet’s pData. extract sample-level metadata data frame . typical cytometry workflow, metadata already available data frame pData; extraction specific HDCytoData’s encoding convention. response group (group_id) derived FCS filenames: R responders NR non-responders. resulting metadata one row per sample (FCS file), columns response status, batch, sample identifier:
code: .meta <-   flowCore::fsApply(     x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1,     FUN = flowCore::exprs,     simplify = FALSE) |>   lapply(FUN = function(x) {          (x[,colnames(x = x) %in%           c("group_id",            "batch_id",            "sample_id")] |>        unique())[1,,drop = TRUE]        }) |>   dplyr::bind_rows(.id = "fcs_file") |>    as.data.frame() |>   dplyr::mutate(     group_id = gsub(pattern = "[[:digit:]]*\\.fcs",                     replacement = "",                     x = fcs_file,                     fixed = FALSE) |>       strsplit(split = "_",                fixed = TRUE) |>       lapply(FUN =  tail,              n = 1) |>       unlist() |>       factor(levels = c("NR", "R")),     batch_id = paste0("batch_",                       batch_id),     sample_id = paste0("sample_",                        sample_id),     ) |>   (\(x)    `rownames<-`(x = x[,colnames(x = x) != "fcs_file"],                 value = x$fcs_file)   )() .meta

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: analysis, prepare flowSet : Removing metadata columns (group_id, batch_id, sample_id) expression data — sample-level annotations, markers. Retaining channels assigned marker class (.e., excluding channels marker_class == "none"). Renaming channels instrument channel names human-readable marker names using MARKER_INFO lookup table.
code: # Remove metadata columns from expression data Krieg_Anti_PD_1 <-   Krieg_Anti_PD_1[,!colnames(x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1) %in%                      c("group_id",                       "batch_id",                       "sample_id")]  # Keep only channels with an assigned marker class Krieg_Anti_PD_1 <-   Krieg_Anti_PD_1[,colnames(x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1) %in%                      MARKER_INFO$channel_name[MARKER_INFO$marker_class != "none"]]  # Rename channels to marker names colnames(x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1) <-   MARKER_INFO$marker_name[     match(x = colnames(x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1),           table = MARKER_INFO$channel_name)]

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: Mass cytometry data typically transformed inverse hyperbolic sine (arcsinh) function using cofactor 5 stabilize variance compress dynamic range. standard transformation CyTOF data.
code: asinhTrans <-   flowCore::arcsinhTransform(     transformationId = "arcsinh",     a = 0,     b = 1/5,     c = 0)  Krieg_Anti_PD_1 <-   flowCore::transform(`_data` = Krieg_Anti_PD_1,                        translist = flowCore::transformList(from = colnames(x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1),                                                           tfun = asinhTrans))

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: attach sample-level metadata flowSet’s pData, tinydenseR uses identify samples covariates.
code: flowCore::pData(object = Krieg_Anti_PD_1) <-   cbind(flowCore::pData(object = Krieg_Anti_PD_1),         .meta[match(x = flowCore::sampleNames(object = Krieg_Anti_PD_1),                     table = rownames(x = .meta)),               , drop = FALSE])

title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: RunTDR() main entry point. creates landmark cells, builds neighborhood graph, computes sample-level density profiles, produces embeddings — single call. cytometry data, set .assay.type = "cyto" provide flowSet directly. .harmony.var = "batch_id" argument instructs tinydenseR apply Harmony batch correction landmark computation, mitigating technical variation processing batches. .sample.var = "name" uses FCS filenames (default sample identifier flowSet pData) define biological samples.
code: lm.cells <-   tinydenseR::RunTDR(     x = Krieg_Anti_PD_1,     .sample.var = "name",     .harmony.var = "batch_id",     .assay.type = "cyto",     .seed = 123, # for reproducibility     .verbose = FALSE   )

what: Step 4: Decomposing the Density Contrast with get.plsD()
title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: Differential density analysis tells us landmark landscape changes occur responders non-responders, marker features drive changes. get.plsD() bridges gap decomposing density contrast PLS components whose loadings reveal markers underlying signal. context cytometry data, loadings correspond protein markers rather gene expression features. set .residualize = TRUE remove residual batch-associated variation decomposition, ensuring plsD components reflect response-related signal rather technical artifacts.
code: lm.cells <-   tinydenseR::get.plsD(     x = lm.cells,      .coef.col = "R",     .model.name = "default",     .verbose = TRUE,     .residualize = TRUE   )

previous_headings: Step 4: Decomposing the Density Contrast with get.plsD() > plsD1: Marker Loadings
title: Cytometry: Anti-PD-1 Response
text: plsD1 decomposition identifies dominant axis marker expression variation covaries density contrast responders non-responders. heatmap rows ranked plsD1 loadings (, contribution component). top positive loadings form coherent myeloid/antigen-presenting program: CD33 (0.61), CD14 (0.60), CD86 (0.53), ICAM-1 (0.52), CD11c (0.50), CD141 (0.48), CD123 (0.45), CD11b (0.45). Notably, HLA-DR positive (0.22) CD16 near-zero loading (0.02) — consistent original finding Krieg et al. CD14+CD16−HLA-DRhi monocytes enriched baseline patients respond anti-PD-1 immunotherapy. negative end plsD1, lymphocyte markers predominate: CD3 (−0.25), CD7 (−0.19), CD62L (−0.17), CD19 (−0.16), CD56 (−0.13). indicates landmarks enriched T, B, NK cell markers tend toward higher density non-responders relative responders along component. Together, plsD1 separates myeloid/monocyte-enriched landmark program (positive loadings, higher density responders) lymphocyte-enriched program (negative loadings, higher density non-responders), providing data-driven decomposition aligns reported predictive role CD14+CD16−HLA-DRhi monocytes anti-PD-1 response.

Nested model embedding: full-model / reduced-model approach get.embedding() isolates sample variation attributable specific variable interest removing confounders. plsD cytometry markers: get.plsD() decomposes density contrasts marker-driven programs. cytometry data, loadings correspond protein markers rather transcriptomic features, providing directly interpretable immune phenotyping information. dataset analyzed — baseline CyTOF profiling melanoma patients anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (Krieg et al., 2018) — well-suited type analysis combines clearly defined clinical variable (response status) high-dimensional immune phenotyping data known biological signal (CD14+CD16−HLA-DRhi monocytes).

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: COMBAT dataset available CELLxGENE (link). original study described : COMBAT Consortium. blood atlas COVID-19 defines hallmarks disease severity specificity. Cell 185, 916–938 (2022).

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
code: library(tinydenseR) library(dplyr) library(forcats) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork) library(limma) library(anndataR) library(BPCells)

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: read COMBAT .h5ad file, keeping data disk via anndataR BPCells memory efficiency. section shows key preprocessing steps; adjust file paths match local setup.
code: # Path to the downloaded h5ad file file_path <- "~/sandbox/COVID19_COMBAT/derived_data/COMBAT.h5ad"  # Access h5ad keeping data on disk h5ad <-    anndataR::read_h5ad(     path = file_path,     as = "HDF5AnnData")  # Extract metadata and feature information feature_metadata <- h5ad$var metadata <- h5ad$obs

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: apply basic QC thresholds restrict analysis healthy volunteers (HV) COVID-19 patients mild, severe, critical disease. Samples fewer 200 cells excluded.
code: # Basic QC: minimum 1000 UMI and 500 genes per cell keep.QC.cells <-    (metadata$QC_total_UMI >= 1000) &    (metadata$QC_ngenes >= 500)  # Keep only HV, MILD, SEV, and CRIT patients with >= 200 cells smpl.to.keep <-   metadata$scRNASeq_sample_ID[     metadata$Source %in% c("HV", "COVID_MILD", "COVID_SEV", "COVID_CRIT")   ] |>    table() |>   (\(x) names(x[x >= 200]))()  metadata <-   metadata[keep.QC.cells & (metadata$scRNASeq_sample_ID %in% smpl.to.keep), ]

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: use BPCells store count matrix disk subset QC-passing cells unique gene symbols.
code: # Remove duplicated gene symbols keep.unique.genes <- !duplicated(feature_metadata$feature_name) feature_metadata <- feature_metadata[keep.unique.genes, ] |> droplevels()  # Write BPCells on-disk matrix ondisk_path <-    "~/sandbox/COVID19_COMBAT/derived_data/COMBAT_COVID_ondisk/"  ondisk.data <-   BPCells::open_matrix_anndata_hdf5(     path = file_path,     group = "/X")  if(!file.exists(path = ondisk_path)){   # write this BPCells matrix to disk   BPCells::write_matrix_dir(     mat = ondisk.data,      dir = ondisk_path) }  # Re-open and subset ondisk.mat <-   BPCells::open_matrix_dir(dir = ondisk_path)  ondisk.mat <-   ondisk.mat[keep.unique.genes, keep.QC.cells & (metadata$scRNASeq_sample_ID %in% smpl.to.keep)]  rownames(x = ondisk.mat) <- feature_metadata$feature_name

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: encode disease severity ordered factor levels binarize time since symptom onset “early” “late” allow non-linear effects. also create combined Source_TSO.binary variable contrast design.
code: metadata <-   metadata |>   dplyr::mutate(     Source = factor(       x = Source,       levels = c("HV", "COVID_MILD", "COVID_SEV", "COVID_CRIT")),     TimeSinceOnset = ifelse(       test = Source != "HV",        yes = TimeSinceOnset,        no = -1)   ) |>   droplevels()

title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: RunTDR() main entry point. creates landmark cells, builds neighborhood graph, computes sample-level density profiles, maps cell type annotations — single call.
code: lm.cells <-   tinydenseR::RunTDR(     x = ondisk.mat,     .sample.var = "scRNASeq_sample_ID",      .cell.meta = metadata,     .assay.type = "RNA",     .celltype.vec = "cell_type",     .label.confidence = 0.5,     .verbose = FALSE,     .seed = 123 # for reproducibility   )

what: Step 2: Visualize Landmark and Sample Embeddings

code: tinydenseR::plotUMAP(   x = lm.cells,   .feature = lm.cells$landmark.annot$celltyping$ids,   .plot.title = "COMBAT landmarks (cell types)" )  +   ggplot2::guides(color = ggplot2::guide_legend(     override.aes = list(size = 5),     ncol = 2)) # Classical monocyte marker p1 <-    tinydenseR::plotUMAP(   x = lm.cells,   .feature = lm.cells$landmarks[, "LYZ"],   .plot.title = "LYZ (classical monocytes)") # MHC class II p2 <-    tinydenseR::plotUMAP(     x = lm.cells,      .feature = lm.cells$landmarks[, "HLA-DRA"],      .plot.title = "HLA-DRA (MHC class II)") # T cell marker p3 <-    tinydenseR::plotUMAP(     x = lm.cells,      .feature = lm.cells$landmarks[, "CD3E"],      .plot.title = "CD3E (T cells)")  p1 | p2 | p3

previous_headings: Step 3: Differential Density Analysis with get.lm()
title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: binarize time since onset “early” vs. “late” create composite factor crosses disease severity timing. allows us define biologically meaningful contrasts controlling confounders.
code: # Binarize TimeSinceOnset lm.cells@metadata <-   dplyr::mutate(     .data = lm.cells@metadata,     TSO.binary =        dplyr::case_when(         Source == "HV" ~ "uninfected",         Source != "HV" ~ ifelse(           TimeSinceOnset > median(TimeSinceOnset[Source != "HV"]),           "late", "early")       ) |>       factor(levels = c("uninfected", "early", "late"))   )  # Standardize Age encoding lm.cells$metadata$Age <-   make.names(lm.cells$metadata$Age) |>   gsub(pattern = "^X", replacement = "Age.", fixed = FALSE)  # Create composite Source x TSO variable lm.cells$metadata$Source_TSO.binary <-   paste(lm.cells$metadata$Source,         lm.cells$metadata$TSO.binary,         sep = "_") |>           gsub(pattern = "^HV_|^COVID_",            replacement = "",            fixed = FALSE) |>             factor(levels = c("uninfected", "MILD_early", "MILD_late", "SEV_early", "SEV_late", "CRIT_early", "CRIT_late"))

previous_headings: Step 3: Differential Density Analysis with get.lm()
title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: set cell-means design ~ 0 + Source_TSO.binary + sex + Age, sex Age controlled every contrast. contrast matrix defines biologically interesting comparisons.
code: # Design matrix (cell-means model controlling for sex and Age) .design <-    model.matrix(~ 0 + Source_TSO.binary + sex + Age,                data = lm.cells$metadata) |>    (\(x) {     colnames(x) <- gsub("^Source_TSO.binary", "", colnames(x))     x   })()  # Contrast matrix .contrast.matrix <-    limma::makeContrasts(     # Critical vs mild within early timepoint     CRIT_earlyVSMILD_early =       CRIT_early - MILD_early,          levels = .design   )

previous_headings: Step 3: Differential Density Analysis with get.lm()
title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: get.lm() fits limma model log-transformed fuzzy density profiles across landmarks, applying contrast matrix. identifies landmarks differentially abundant conditions — analogous finding differentially expressed genes bulk RNA-seq, level cell density.
code: lm.cells <-    tinydenseR::get.lm(     x = lm.cells,     .design = .design,     .contrasts = .contrast.matrix,     .model.name = "contrast.Source_TSO.binary",     .verbose = TRUE   )

what: plsD1: Inflammatory Myeloid Program vs. Interferon Response
title: scRNA-seq: COVID-19 PBMCs
text: plsD1 captures dominant signal distinguishing early critical mild COVID-19. Landmarks monocyte region carry strongest plsD1 scores, indicating component driven monocyte-state variation.  left panel shows landmark scores: landmarks monocyte region carry strongest positive plsD1 scores, indicating component driven monocyte-state variation. right panel confirms tight coupling plsD1 scores density contrast (Y). plsD1 gene loadings identify molecular signatures underlying signal. Positive loadings indicate features upregulated direction contrast (CRIT > MILD), negative loadings indicate downregulation.  #### Interpretation Positive plsD1 loadings enriched neutrophil degranulation, key contributing genes including S100A12, CLU, VCAN, CTSD. Negative loadings enriched interferon gamma response interferon alpha response programs, driven genes IFIT3, IFIT1, IFITM1, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1. axis consistent shift interferon-responsive classical monocytes early mild disease toward inflammatory, dysregulated myeloid program early critical disease, line previous reports myeloid dysregulation severe COVID-19.
code: tinydenseR::plotPlsD(   x = lm.cells,   .coef.col = "CRIT_earlyVSMILD_early",   .plsD.dim = 1 ) tinydenseR::plotPlsDHeatmap(   x = lm.cells,   .coef.col = "CRIT_earlyVSMILD_early",   .plsD.dim = 1,   .order.by = "plsD.dim",   .feature.font.size = I(x = 5) ) # enrichment analysis setup msigdb.H <-   msigdbr::msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens")  hypotheses.1 <-   c(     "REACTOME_NEUTROPHIL_DEGRANULATION",     "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_ALPHA_RESPONSE",     "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE"     ) |>    (\(x)    unique(x = x[x %in% msigdb.H$gs_name])    )()    msigdb.list.1 <-   msigdb.H[msigdb.H$gs_name %in% hypotheses.1,            c("gene_symbol",              "gs_name")] |>   (\(x)    split(x = x$gene_symbol,          f = x$gs_name)   )()  |>   lapply(FUN = unique)  set.seed(seed = 123) fgseaRes.plsD1 <-    fgsea::fgseaMultilevel(pathways = msigdb.list.1,                           stats = lm.cells$results$pls$CRIT_earlyVSMILD_early$loadings[,"plsD1"],                          eps = 1e-50,                          minSize = 5,                          maxSize = 500,                          nPermSimple = 1e5)  # add concordance score for leading edge genes (the higher the score, the higher the confidence in the results) fgseaRes.plsD1$concordance <-   lapply(X = fgseaRes.plsD1$leadingEdge,          FUN = function(lead.edge){            mean(x = lm.cells$results$pls$CRIT_earlyVSMILD_early$concordance.weights[lead.edge,"plsD1"])          }) |>   unlist()  fgseaRes.plsD1[order(-log10(x = padj)*sign(NES)),]  (p <-   names(x = msigdb.list.1) |>   lapply(FUN = function(pw) {          fgsea::plotEnrichment(pathway = msigdb.list.1[[pw]],                           stats =  lm.cells$results$pls$CRIT_earlyVSMILD_early$loadings[,"plsD1"]) +       ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5,                                                         size = I(x = 12)),                      plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5,                                                            size = I(x = 10)),       ) +       ggplot2::labs(         title = gsub(pattern = "_",                                  replacement = " ",                                  x = pw,                                  fixed = TRUE) |>             gsub(pattern = "^HALLMARK |^REACTOME ",                     replacement = "",                   fixed = FALSE) |>                       tolower(),                     subtitle = paste0("p = ",                                       formatC(x = fgseaRes.plsD1[pathway == pw, padj],                                               digits = 0,                                               format = "e"))) +       ggh4x::force_panelsizes(rows = grid::unit(x = 1,                                                 units = "in"),                               col = grid::unit(x = 1.5,                                                units = "in"))   }) |>   c(nrow = 1) |>     do.call(what = gridExtra::grid.arrange))

title: Multi-Backend Workflows with tinydenseR
text: dispatch mechanism follows simple three-step pattern. call analysis function container object (e.g., Seurat object), S3 method: Extracts TDRObj GetTDR() Runs computation TDRObj Stores updated TDRObj back SetTDR() TDRObj stored depends container: Note: H5AD matrix-class inputs always produce bare TDRObj, GetTDR/SetTDR needed backends.
code: User calls:  get.lm(seurat_obj, .design = design)                 |                 v S3 dispatch --> get.lm.Seurat(x, ...)                 |                 +-- tdr <- GetTDR(x)            # Extract TDRObj from Misc slot                 +-- tdr <- get.lm.TDRObj(tdr, ...)  # Run computation                 +-- SetTDR(x, tdr)              # Store updated TDRObj back                 |                 v Returns:     updated seurat_obj (with results inside)

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: tinydenseR landmark-based framework differential abundance differential expression analysis single-cell data. designed scRNA-seq, flow, mass, spectral cytometry data. core idea select representative set landmark cells, build graph , map cells nearest landmarks, model per-landmark density expression across samples biological replicates. preserves statistical rigor maintaining richness single-cell resolution.

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: analysis results stored single S4 class, TDRObj. contains 12 slots organize data results stage pipeline: $ accessor overloaded can use tdr$results instead tdr@results:
code: tdr$metadata              # sample-level metadata tdr$landmark.embed$pca    # PCA coordinates of landmarks tdr$results$lm            # linear model results tdr$config$assay.type     # "RNA" or "cyto"

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: standard analysis pipeline proceeds series stages, populating specific slots TDRObj: typical workflow looks like:
code: setup.tdr.obj() / RunTDR()     |     v get.landmarks()  --> landmark.embed (pca, le)     |                 assay (raw, expr, scaled)     v get.graph()      --> graphs (adj.matrix, snn)     |                 landmark.embed (umap)     |                 landmark.annot (clustering)     v get.map()        --> density (raw, norm, log.norm, composition)     |                 cellmap (nearest.lm, fuzzy.graphs)     v get.lm()         --> results$lm (fit, trad)     |                 sample.embed (pca)     v get.pbDE()            --> results$pb get.pbDE(.mode="marker") --> results$marker get.plsD()           --> results$pls library(tinydenseR)  # Option 1: step-by-step tdr <- setup.tdr.obj(...) tdr <- get.landmarks(tdr) tdr <- get.graph(tdr) tdr <- get.map(tdr) tdr <- get.lm(tdr, .design = design)  # Option 2: RunTDR() runs landmarks + graph + map + embedding in one call result <- RunTDR(seurat_obj, .sample.var = "sample_id", .assay.type = "RNA") result <- get.lm(result, .design = design)

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: tinydenseR uses S3 dispatch support multiple container types (Seurat, SingleCellExperiment) single API. H5AD inputs (either file path via anndataR) converted bare TDRObj RunTDR() carry container dispatch methods. dispatch wrappers dispatch.R follow one three patterns:

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: Functions need TDRObj computation. wrapper extracts TDRObj, runs .TDRObj method, stores back. Functions tier: get.graph(), get.lm(), get.embedding(), get.plsD(), get.features(), lm.cluster(), celltyping().
code: # Example: get.lm.Seurat get.lm.Seurat <- function(x, ...) {   tdr <- GetTDR(x)   tdr <- get.lm.TDRObj(tdr, ...)   SetTDR(x, tdr) }

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: Functions need access original data container (e.g., read expression matrices via .get_sample_matrix()). source object passed via .source argument. Functions tier: get.landmarks(), get.map(), get.pbDE(). Note: get.markerDE() soft-deprecated. Use get.pbDE(.mode = "marker") instead. get.pbDE() function supports two modes: .mode = "design" (default) — pseudobulk differential expression .mode = "marker" — marker gene/protein identification groups
code: # Example: get.landmarks.Seurat get.landmarks.Seurat <- function(x, ...) {   tdr <- GetTDR(x)   tdr <- get.landmarks.TDRObj(tdr, .source = x, ...)   SetTDR(x, tdr) }

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: goi.summary() read-Tier 2 function: needs .source expression access, returns summary data frame rather updating container.
code: # Example: goi.summary.Seurat goi.summary.Seurat <- function(x, ...) {   tdr <- GetTDR(x)   goi.summary.TDRObj(tdr, .source = x, ...) }

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: Plot functions need read TDRObj; return plot object rather updating container. plot*() functions follow pattern: plotPCA(), plotUMAP(), plotBeeswarm(), plot2Markers(), plotSamplePCA(), plotSampleEmbedding(), plotTradStats(), plotTradPerc(), plotDensity(), plotPbDE(), plotDEA(), plotMarkerDE(), plotHeatmap(), plotPlsD(), plotPlsDHeatmap().
code: # Example: plotPCA.Seurat plotPCA.Seurat <- function(x, ...) plotPCA.TDRObj(GetTDR(x), ...)

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: tinydenseR needs access per-sample expression matrices pipeline, calls internal function .get_sample_matrix(). function dispatches config$backend retrieve data appropriate source: "matrix" backend used dgCMatrix, DelayedMatrix, IterableMatrix, H5AD inputs (RunTDR.character RunTDR.HDF5AnnData). cases, reference matrix stored locked environment within config$source.env .get_sample_matrix() can slice without copying entire matrix. "seurat" "sce" backends, live container object passed .source Tier 2 functions, forward .get_sample_matrix().

title: tinydenseR Package Architecture
text: tinydenseR includes -disk caching system per-cell mapping results (nearest landmark assignments, fuzzy graph memberships). avoids recomputing expensive cell--landmark mappings re-running downstream analyses. cache managed three user-facing functions: Internal helpers (.tdr_cache_read(), .tdr_cache_write(), .tdr_cache_sweep_orphans()) handle low-level read/write operations intended called directly users.
code: # Show cache location and size tdr_cache_info(tdr)  # Validate cache integrity tdr_cache_validate(tdr)  # Remove orphaned cache files tdr_cache_cleanup(tdr)

title: Linking Cell-To-Cell Variation to Sample-to-Sample Variation
text: tinydenseR requires R (>= 4.1) Bioconductor CRAN packages. dependencies installed automatically, may need install Bioconductor dependencies first: can install tinydenseR GitHub using devtools:
code: if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("BiocManager") }  # Download DESCRIPTION from GitHub data_url <-   "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Novartis/tinydenseR/main/DESCRIPTION" temp_file <-   tempfile() utils::download.file(   url = data_url,    destfile = temp_file,    mode = "wb",   quiet = TRUE)  # Parse Imports desc <-   read.dcf(file = temp_file) imports <-   strsplit(x = desc[, "Imports"],            split = "\\s*,\\s*")[[1]] imports <-   gsub(pattern = "\\s*\\(.*?\\)",         replacement = "",         x = imports)  # remove version constraints  # Install only missing Bioconductor packages avail.bioc.pkgs <-   BiocManager::repositories() |>   (\(x)   available.packages(repos = x)   )() |>   rownames()   bioc_pkgs <-   imports[imports %in%             avail.bioc.pkgs[!avail.bioc.pkgs %in%                                (installed.packages() |>                                  rownames())]] if (length(bioc_pkgs) > 0) {   BiocManager::install(pkgs = bioc_pkgs,                        update = FALSE) }  unlink(temp_file) # Install devtools if you haven't already if (!require("devtools")) install.packages("devtools")  # Install `tinydenseR` devtools::install_github("Novartis/tinydenseR")

Condition effects introduced systematically varying proportion cells assigned condition trajectory milestone, creating differential cell-state abundance along trajectory.

title: Linking Cell-To-Cell Variation to Sample-to-Sample Variation
text: RunTDR() selects landmark cells, builds nearest-neighbor graph, clusters landmarks, maps cells nearest landmarks generate landmark--sample matrix single call. Unsupervised sample embedding also returned automatically.
code: sim_trajectory <-   tinydenseR::RunTDR(     x = sim_trajectory,     .sample.var = "Sample",     .assay.type = "RNA",     .nHVG = 500,     .verbose = FALSE,     .seed = 123 # for reproducibility   ) #> Loading required namespace: SingleCellExperiment #> Warning in (function (A, nv = 5, nu = nv, maxit = 1000, work = nv + 7, reorth = #> TRUE, : You're computing too large a percentage of total singular values, use a #> standard svd instead.

title: Linking Cell-To-Cell Variation to Sample-to-Sample Variation
text: set design matrix test landmarks show differential density conditions.
code: .design <-   model.matrix(~ Condition,                data = tinydenseR::GetTDR(sim_trajectory)@metadata)  sim_trajectory <-   tinydenseR::get.lm(     x = sim_trajectory,     .design = .design,     .verbose = FALSE   ) #> Warning in get.lm.TDRObj(tdr, ...): PCA-weighted q-value estimation is not #> recommended for fewer than 1000 tests. Using standard q-value estimation #> instead. #> Warning in value[[3L]](cond): q-value estimation failed. Using BH instead. #> Warning in get.lm.TDRObj(tdr, ...): Only 1 cluster detected. Skipping limma fit #> on cluster composition (nothing to compare). Density-level analysis (get.plsD, #> get.dea, etc.) is unaffected.

title: Linking Cell-To-Cell Variation to Sample-to-Sample Variation
text: fit reduced (intercept-) model compute partial-effect PCs produce quantitative per-sample score along Condition axis.  unsupervised PCA shows inter-sample variation based density profiles across landmarks. supervised partial-effect PC isolates variation along Condition axis, clearly separating two groups.
code: # Reduced model (no Condition term) noCondition.design <-   model.matrix(~ 1,                data = tinydenseR::GetTDR(sim_trajectory)@metadata)  sim_trajectory <-   tinydenseR::get.lm(     x = sim_trajectory,     .design = noCondition.design,     .model.name = "noCondition",     .verbose = FALSE   ) #> Warning in get.lm.TDRObj(tdr, ...): PCA-weighted q-value estimation is not #> recommended for fewer than 1000 tests. Using standard q-value estimation #> instead. #> Warning in get.lm.TDRObj(tdr, ...): Only 1 cluster detected. Skipping limma fit #> on cluster composition (nothing to compare). Density-level analysis (get.plsD, #> get.dea, etc.) is unaffected.  # Compute partial-effect PCs sim_trajectory <-   tinydenseR::get.embedding(     x = sim_trajectory,     .full.model = "default",     .red.model = "noCondition",     .term.of.interest = "Condition",     .verbose = FALSE   ) # Unsupervised sample PCA smpl.pca <-   tinydenseR::plotSampleEmbedding(     x = sim_trajectory,     .embedding = "pca",     .color.by = "Condition",     .cat.feature.color = tinydenseR::Color.Palette[1, c(1, 2)],     .panel.size = 1.5,     .point.size = 3   ) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "PCA") +   ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5),                  legend.position = "bottom")  # Supervised partial-effect PC smpl.pePC <-   tinydenseR::plotSampleEmbedding(     x = sim_trajectory,     .embedding = "pePC",     .sup.embed.slot = "Condition",     .color.by = "Condition",     .cat.feature.color = tinydenseR::Color.Palette[1, c(1, 2)],     .panel.size = 1.5,     .point.size = 3   ) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "partial-effect PC") +   ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5),                  legend.position = "bottom")  (smpl.pca | smpl.pePC) +   patchwork::plot_layout(guides = "collect") &   ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "bottom",                  legend.justification = "center")

title: Linking Cell-To-Cell Variation to Sample-to-Sample Variation
text: decompose density contrast interpretable gene-expression programs using get.plsD(), visualize result patchwork density fold-change plsD1 scores landmark PCA.  left panel shows density log2 fold-change landmark: landmarks enriched Condition B red, enriched blue. right panel shows plsD1 scores, capture leading gene-expression program aligned density contrast.
code: sim_trajectory <-   tinydenseR::get.plsD(     x = sim_trajectory,     .coef.col = "ConditionB",     .model.name = "default",     .verbose = FALSE   ) # Density fold-change on landmark PCA dens.p <-   tinydenseR::plotPCA(     x = sim_trajectory,     .feature = tinydenseR::GetTDR(sim_trajectory)@results$lm$default$fit$coefficients[, "ConditionB"],     .panel.size = 1.5,     .point.size = 1,     .color.label = "density\nlog2(+0.5)FC",     .midpoint = 0,     .legend.position = "bottom"   ) +   ggplot2::guides(color = ggplot2::guide_colorbar(title.position = "top",                                                   title.hjust = 0.5)) +   ggplot2::theme(plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_blank(),                  legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = -0.1, unit = "in"))  # plsD1 scores on landmark PCA plsD1.p <-   tinydenseR::plotPlsD(     x = sim_trajectory,     .coef.col = "ConditionB",     .plsD.dim = 1,     .embed = "pca",     .panel.size = 1.5   )[[1]]  # Patchwork: density contrast | plsD1 ((dens.p +     ggplot2::labs(title = "") +     ggplot2::theme(legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = -0.1,                                                    unit = "in"))) |    (plsD1.p +       ggplot2::labs(title = "") +       ggplot2::theme(legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 0.1,                                                      unit = "in")))) +   patchwork::plot_annotation(title = "Density contrast: Condition B vs A") &   ggplot2::theme(     plot.title =       ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5,                             margin = ggplot2::margin(t = -0.1,                                                      unit = "in")))

title: Linking Cell-To-Cell Variation to Sample-to-Sample Variation
text: plsD heatmap shows landmark expression profiles ordered plsD1 scores, gene loadings right. Rows display centered expression top 25 features associated direction plsD1.  tinydenseR recovered trajectory-associated cell-state changes, embedded samples along variable interest, uncovered gene-expression programs associated changes cell density, illustrating ability model continuous cellular molecular variation without relying hard cluster boundaries.
code: tinydenseR::plotPlsDHeatmap(   x = sim_trajectory,   .coef.col = "ConditionB",   .plsD.dim = 1,   .order.by = "plsD.dim",   .panel.height = 3,   .feature.font.size = 4 )

title: Run the full tinydenseR pipeline — RunTDR
text: x DelayedMatrix (features × cells RNA). ... Additional arguments passed .run_tdr_matrix (e.g. .sample.var, .assay.type, .harmony.var, .markers, .celltype.vec, .min.cells.per.sample, .verbose, .seed, .prop.landmarks, .n.threads). .celltype.vec Character(1). Column name .cell.meta containing per-cell type labels, NULL. .celltype.vec.overwrite Logical. TRUE, replace existing .celltype.vec stored TDRObj config. .verbose Logical. Print progress messages. .seed Integer. Random seed. .sample.var Character(1). Column name .cell.meta identifying sample membership. .assay Character(1). Name assay assayNames(x). .layer Character(1). Layer within assay (e.g. "counts"). .assay.type Character. "RNA" "cyto". .harmony.var Character vector batch variable column names sample-level metadata, NULL. .markers Character vector marker names (required cyto). .min.cells.per.sample Integer. Minimum cells sample included. .prop.landmarks Numeric (0, 1]. Proportion cells landmarks. .n.threads Integer. Number threads. .bpcells.dir Character(1) NULL. Directory path BPCells -disk matrix. NULL (default), uses temporary directory (tempdir()) cleaned session end. path given already contains valid BPCells matrix, conversion step skipped (cache hit). .h5ad.group Character(1) NULL. HDF5 group path containing count matrix (e.g. "/layers/counts" "/X"). NULL (default), auto-detects probing /layers/counts first, falling back /X. .cell.meta data.frame per-cell metadata. Must one row per cell rownames matching cell IDs x (colnames RNA, rownames cyto).

title: Run the full tinydenseR pipeline — RunTDR
text: RunTDR convenience wrapper around explicit step--step workflow (get.meta → get.cells → setup.tdr.obj → get.landmarks → get.graph → get.map → get.embedding). equivalent inputs, seed, arguments two paths produce numerically identical results.

title: Run the full tinydenseR pipeline — RunTDR
text: RunTDR(default): Run pipeline existing TDRObj Executes get.landmarks → get.graph → celltyping → get.map → get.embedding pre-built TDRObj. RunTDR(Seurat): Run pipeline directly Seurat object Builds TDRObj Seurat object executes full pipeline. finished TDRObj stored SeuratObject::Misc(x, slot = "tdr.obj"). categorical cell-level columns x@meta.data automatically imported named celltyping solutions via import_cell_annotations.  .celltype.vec column (specified) set active annotation. RunTDR(SingleCellExperiment): Run pipeline directly SingleCellExperiment Builds TDRObj SingleCellExperiment executes full pipeline. finished TDRObj stored S4Vectors::metadata(x)$tdr.obj. assay DelayedMatrix (e.g., HDF5-backed), converted BPCells -disk IterableMatrix routed .run_tdr_matrix() efficient lazy access. assay -memory matrix (e.g., dgCMatrix), existing SCE backend path used. RunTDR(HDF5AnnData): Run pipeline HDF5AnnData object Builds TDRObj HDF5-backed AnnData object. expression matrix converted BPCells -disk directory efficient lazy access, proven IterableMatrix pipeline used downstream steps. Metadata (obs) read via anndataR; expression matrix read converted via BPCells. RunTDR(character): Run pipeline directly h5ad file path Reads metadata gene/cell names h5ad file using rhdf5, opens expression matrix BPCells, delegates internal IterableMatrix pipeline. method supports H5AD format versions, including pre-0.8.0 files generated older versions Python anndata supported anndataR package. RunTDR(cytoset): Run pipeline flowWorkspace cytoset Builds TDRObj cytoset object (one FCS sample per cytoframe) executes full pipeline. RunTDR(flowSet): Run pipeline flowCore flowSet Builds TDRObj flowSet object (one FCS sample per flowFrame) executes full pipeline. Requires flowCore (flowWorkspace). RunTDR(dgCMatrix): Run pipeline sparse matrix (dgCMatrix) Builds TDRObj dgCMatrix per-cell metadata, executes full pipeline. RunTDR(DelayedMatrix): Run pipeline DelayedMatrix Converts DelayedMatrix BPCells -disk IterableMatrix efficient lazy access, delegates proven IterableMatrix pipeline via .run_tdr_matrix(). RunTDR(IterableMatrix): Run pipeline BPCells IterableMatrix

title: TDRObj: S4 class for tinydenseR analysis objects — TDRObj-class
text: cells list. Named list per-sample file paths expression matrices. metadata data.frame. Sample-level metadata. config list. Run parameters: key, sampling, assay.type, markers, n.threads. integration list. Trained projection models batch variables (harmony.var, harmony.obj, symphony.obj, umap.model). assay list. Landmark expression layers (L x features matrices): raw (raw counts), expr (normalized/log expression), scaled (Z-scored). landmark.embed list. Landmark-space coordinate matrices; entry $coord slot. Contains pca, le, umap sub-lists. landmark.annot list. Per-landmark categorical annotations (factor, length L). Contains clustering celltyping sub-lists, $ids factor. graphs list. Landmark-landmark connectivity matrices: adj.matrix, snn, fgraph. density list. Fuzzy density analytics populated get.map. Contains five sub-elements: raw L × N matrix pre-normalization fuzzy density sums. norm L × N matrix size-factor normalization: norm = t(t(raw) / size.factors). log.norm L × N matrix: log2(norm + 0.5). size.factors Named numeric(N): \(n_j / \bar{n}\), guaranteed mean 1. composition List clustering/celltyping cell count percentage matrices (samples × clusters). Access via get.density $ accessor. sample.embed list. Sample-level embeddings (N x k matrices), $coord. Contains pca, traj, pepc sub-lists. cellmap list. Per-cell, per-sample data unified structure: clustering$ids, celltyping$ids (named per-sample lists optional named solutions), nearest.lm, fuzzy.graphs.  sample entry either -memory R object attributed path string -disk cache. results list. statistical outputs: lm, pb, marker, spec, nmf, pls, features.

title: Find elbow point using second derivative method (internal) — elbow.sec.deriv
text: x Numeric vector values (e.g., eigenvalues, explained variance). Must least 4 values second derivative calculation. smooth Logical, whether apply smoothing spline computing second derivative. Default TRUE. applied length(x) > 6. df Degrees freedom smoothing spline. NULL (default), auto-calculated min(length(x) * 0.7, 10). Lower values = smoother fit. sort.order Character, either "asc" "desc". Specifies expected ordering meaningful values: "asc" - values increase importance (Laplacian Eigenmaps) "desc" - values decrease importance (PCA eigenvalues) algorithm works sorted data, ensures correct interpretation.

title: Find elbow point using second derivative method (internal) — elbow.sec.deriv
text: List three elements: index Integer index elbow point original x vector value Numeric value x elbow point sec.deriv Maximum second derivative value (diagnostic)

title: Find elbow point using second derivative method (internal) — elbow.sec.deriv
text: algorithm: Sorts values according sort.order Optionally smooths spline reduce noise Normalizes [0,1] range scale-invariance Computes second derivative using diff(..., differences = 2) Returns index second derivative largest Smoothing recommended noisy data requires least 7 points. df parameter controls smoothing strength: lower values = smoother.

title: Create .cells Object from Seurat v5 Object — get.cells.Seurat5
text: .seurat.obj Seurat v5 object containing layered RNA data. Must use Assay5 class. .meta Data frame sample-level metadata. Rownames must sample IDs matching values .seurat.obj@meta.data[[.sample.var]]. Required filtering valid samples. .sample.var Character: column name .seurat.obj@meta.data identifying sample cell belongs (e.g., "sample_id", "Sample", "orig.ident"). .assay Character: assay name extract. Default "RNA". .layer.pattern Character: pattern match layer names. Default "counts" matches layers containing count data (e.g., "counts.Sample1", "counts.Sample2"). title: Access fuzzy density matrices from a TDRObj — get.density
text: x TDRObj (Seurat / SCE / HDF5AnnData wrapping one via GetTDR). ... Additional arguments passed methods. .Character(1). density layer return: "raw" Pre-size-factor-normalization fuzzy density sums (landmarks × samples). entry \(\sum_c F_j[c,l]\), total fuzzy edge weight connecting sample \(j\)'s cells landmark \(l\). "norm" Size-factor-normalized fuzzy density (landmarks × samples). Equals raw / size.factors (column-wise). primary analytical layer. Aliases: "fdens" (deprecated). "log.norm" Log2-transformed normalized density: log2(norm + 0.5). Used get.lm() get.embedding(). Aliases: "Y" (deprecated).

title: Access fuzzy density matrices from a TDRObj — get.density

title: Access fuzzy density matrices from a TDRObj — get.density
text: three density layers form deterministic chain computed get.map: $$\mathrm{raw}[l,j] = \sum_{c \j} F_j[c,l]$$ \(F_j\) fuzzy graph (UMAP edge weights) sample \(j\), \(c\) indexes cells, \(l\) indexes landmarks. $$\mathrm{norm} = t(t(\mathrm{raw}) \;/\; \mathrm{size.factors})$$ \(\mathrm{size.factors}[j] = n_j / \bar{n}\) (cells per sample divided mean cell count across samples). $$\mathrm{log.norm} = \log_2(\mathrm{norm} + 0.5)$$ pseudo-count 0.5 stabilizes landmarks zero density.

title: Compute Sample Embedding from Partial Fitted Values — get.embedding
text: x TDRObj, Seurat, SingleCellExperiment, HDF5AnnData (anndataR) object processed get.map(). Contains @density$log.norm (log2-transformed densities) used unsupervised embeddings. Statistical model fits stored @results$lm via get.lm(). ... Additional arguments passed methods. .full.model Character string naming full model .tdr.obj$map$lm (default "default"). Required computing supervised embeddings (pePC). .term..interest Character string: covariate/term isolated using nested model method (.red.model). Must match column name .tdr.obj$metadata. Ignored using .contrast..interest. .red.model Optional character string naming reduced model .tdr.obj$map$lm (without effect interest). provided, computes \(\Delta\hat{Y} = \hat{Y}_{full} - \hat{Y}_{red}\) directly. Make sure construct nested models "dropping terms" (reduced strict subset full)! .contrast..interest Optional: Character string naming contrast extract. Must match column name full model's contrasts. specified, uses Frisch-Waugh-Lovell (FWL) theorem compute true partial fitted component. .n.eigs Integer: number eigenvectors diffusion map (default 20). .n.pcs Integer: number PCs unsupervised PCA diffusion map (default 20). .ret.trajectory Logical: whether compute diffusion map trajectory embedding (default FALSE). .traj.dist.metric Character: distance metric diffusion map (default "cosine"). .seed Integer: random seed reproducibility sample-level PCA (via irlba::prcomp_irlba) diffusion map computation. Default 123. .verbose Logical: print progress messages? Default TRUE.

title: Compute Sample Embedding from Partial Fitted Values — get.embedding
text: Modified .tdr.obj embeddings stored .tdr.obj$map$embedding: pca Unsupervised PCA log-transformed landmark densities: coord: Sample coordinates (samples x PCs) rotation: Loadings (landmarks x PCs) center, scale, sdev: Centering, scaling, standard deviations traj Diffusion map trajectory embedding: coord: Sample coordinates (samples x DCs) eigenvalues, eigenvectors: Raw diffusion map components sdev: sqrt(abs(eigenvalues)) pePC Supervised partial-effect embeddings (pePC args provided): <contrast> <term>: Named list containing: coord: Nuisance-residualized projection (samples x pePCs) x: PCA scores partial fitted values rotation: Landmark loadings partial-effect PCA effect.resid.cor: Per-PC correlation diagnostic delta.Yhat: Partial fitted values matrix method: "fwl_contrast" "nested_models"

title: Compute Sample Embedding from Partial Fitted Values — get.embedding
text: Unsupervised Embeddings (always computed): Method 1: FWL-based contrast extraction (.contrast..interest provided) cell-means model nuisance covariates, uses Frisch-Waugh-Lovell theorem: Compute contrast regressor: \(x_c = X_{group} \cdot c\) Residualize nuisance: \(x_{c,\perp} = (- H_Z) x_c\) Residualize Y nuisance: \(Y_\perp = (- H_Z) Y\) Compute partial coefficient: \(\gamma_g = (x_{c,\perp}' Y_{\perp,g}) / (x_{c,\perp}' x_{c,\perp})\) Form \(\Delta\hat{Y} = \gamma_g \otimes x_{c,\perp}\) Method 2: Nested model comparison (.red.model provided) Direct computation: \(\Delta\hat{Y} = \hat{Y}_{full} - \hat{Y}_{red}\) method useful want isolate effect one covariates without using contrasts (e.g., comparing ~ disease + sex + age vs ~ sex + age extract disease effect). Embedding via PCA: embedding computed two steps: Learn PCA basis \(V\) \(\Delta\hat{Y}^\top\) (partial fitted values): pca <- prcomp(t(delta.Yhat), center = TRUE, scale. = FALSE, rank. = r) Project nuisance-residualized samples onto basis: coord <- t(Y - Yhat.red - pca$center) %*% pca$rotation \(E_{red} = Y - \hat{Y}_{red}\) nuisance-residualized features (remains removing nuisance effects). loadings \(V\) encode linear subspace (often rank-1 single contrast) maximizes variance partial-effect matrix \(\Delta\hat{Y}^\top\). Geometrically, evaluates residualized samples align effect direction(s). rank-1 effects (single contrast two-level factor), scores increase residuals align unique partial-effect axis. rank > 1 effects, pePC axis captures variance-maximizing direction within multi-dimensional effect subspace (see ).

title: Compute Sample Embedding from Partial Fitted Values — get.embedding
text: dropped term rank > 1 (e.g., factor two levels), nested model method (.red.model) produces \(\Delta\hat{Y}\) rank \(r = p_{full} - p_{red} > 1\). PCA learns \(r\) orthogonal axes maximize variance within effect subspace. Key property: axes rotations maximize explained variance. correspond individual level-vs-baseline comparisons. example, three-level factor (Baseline, D1, D7), nested model pePC produces two axes (pePC1, pePC2) span subspace D1-vs-Baseline D7-vs-Baseline effects, pePC1 might capture "shared activation" pePC2 captures difference D1 D7. whole-term embedding correct parameterization-invariant: \(\Delta\hat{Y}\) depends column spaces full reduced design matrices, choice factor coding (treatment, sum, Helmert) reference level. pePC subspace faithfully represents complete effect dropped term. per-level interpretability, use explicit contrasts instead. FWL-based method (.contrast..interest) produces rank-1 embeddings isolate specific comparisons (e.g., D1 vs Baseline, D7 vs Baseline). requires fitting cell-means model limma::makeContrasts. See Example 3 . Practical guidance: Two-level factors single contrasts: methods equivalent produce single, interpretable pePC axis. Multi-level factors, whole-term view: use nested models. Useful omnibus visualization ("factor matter ?") downstream analyses operate full effect subspace. Multi-level factors, per-level view: use explicit contrasts. call get.embedding() different .contrast..interest produces separate rank-1 embedding clear biological interpretation.

title: Compute Sample Embedding from Partial Fitted Values — get.embedding
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # =========================================================================== # Example 0: Unsupervised embeddings only (before get.lm) # ===========================================================================  # After get.map, compute unsupervised embeddings lm.obj <- get.embedding(.tdr.obj = lm.obj)  # Access unsupervised embeddings lm.obj$map$embedding$pca$coord   # sample PCA coordinates lm.obj$map$embedding$traj$coord  # sample diffusion map coordinates  # Plot unsupervised embeddings plotSampleEmbedding(lm.obj, .embedding = "pca", .color.by = "Condition") plotSampleEmbedding(lm.obj, .embedding = "traj", .color.by = "Timepoint")  # =========================================================================== # Example 1: Nested model comparison (no contrasts) # ===========================================================================  # Fit full model with disease effect (stored as "full") .design <- model.matrix(object = ~ disease + sex + age,                         data = lm.obj$metadata) lm.obj <- get.lm(.tdr.obj = lm.obj, .design = .design,                      .model.name = "full")  # Fit reduced model without disease (stored as "reduced") .red.design <- model.matrix(object = ~ sex + age,                             data = lm.obj$metadata) lm.obj <- get.lm(.tdr.obj = lm.obj, .design = .red.design,                      .model.name = "reduced")  # Extract disease effect embedding (all stored in lm.obj) lm.obj <- get.embedding(     .tdr.obj = lm.obj,     .full.model = "full",     .term.of.interest = "disease",     .red.model = "reduced" )  # Plot the embedding (colored by disease) plotSampleEmbedding(lm.obj, .embedding = "pePC", .sup.embed.slot = "disease")  # =========================================================================== # Example 2: FWL-based contrast extraction (cell-means model) # ===========================================================================  # Fit cell-means model with contrasts design <- model.matrix(~ 0 + Group + Batch + Age, data = lm.obj$metadata) contrasts <- limma::makeContrasts(     TrtVsCtrl = GroupTrt - GroupCtrl,     KO_TrtVsCtrl = GroupKO_Trt - GroupKO_Ctrl,     levels = design ) lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = design, .contrasts = contrasts)  # Extract each contrast embedding lm.obj <- get.embedding(lm.obj, .contrast.of.interest = "TrtVsCtrl") lm.obj <- get.embedding(lm.obj, .contrast.of.interest = "KO_TrtVsCtrl")  # Plot the embedding (specify .color.by for metadata column) plotSampleEmbedding(lm.obj, .embedding = "pePC",                     .sup.embed.slot = "TrtVsCtrl", .color.by = "Group")  # Access the embedding components (all in lm.obj$map$embedding) lm.obj$map$embedding$pca$coord              # sample PCA coordinates lm.obj$map$embedding$traj$coord             # sample trajectory coordinates lm.obj$map$embedding$pePC$TrtVsCtrl$coord   # sample pePC coordinates lm.obj$map$embedding$pePC$TrtVsCtrl$rotation # landmark loadings lm.obj$map$embedding$pePC$TrtVsCtrl$delta.Yhat # partial fitted values  # Multiple embeddings stored together names(lm.obj$map$embedding)       # c("pca", "traj", "pePC") names(lm.obj$map$embedding$pePC)  # c("TrtVsCtrl", "KO_TrtVsCtrl")  # =========================================================================== # Example 3: Multi-level factor — per-level embeddings via explicit contrasts # ===========================================================================  # Suppose Timepoint has three levels: Baseline, D1, D7. # # Option A (nested models): whole-term embedding with rank 2. # pePC1 and pePC2 are variance-maximizing rotations within the # Timepoint effect subspace — they do NOT correspond to individual # level-vs-baseline comparisons.  full.design  <- model.matrix(~ Timepoint + Batch, data = lm.obj$metadata) red.design   <- model.matrix(~ Batch, data = lm.obj$metadata) lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = full.design,  .model.name = "tp_full") lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = red.design,   .model.name = "tp_red")  lm.obj <- get.embedding(lm.obj,     .full.model = "tp_full", .red.model = "tp_red",     .term.of.interest = "Timepoint") # -> produces pePC1, pePC2: omnibus Timepoint effect  # Option B (explicit contrasts): one rank-1 embedding per comparison. # Each pePC axis directly captures a specific level-vs-baseline effect.  cm.design <- model.matrix(~ 0 + Timepoint + Batch, data = lm.obj$metadata) tp.contrasts <- limma::makeContrasts(     D1vsBaseline = TimepointD1  - TimepointBaseline,     D7vsBaseline = TimepointD7  - TimepointBaseline,     levels = cm.design ) lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = cm.design,                   .contrasts = tp.contrasts,                   .model.name = "tp_contrasts")  lm.obj <- get.embedding(lm.obj,     .full.model = "tp_contrasts",     .contrast.of.interest = "D1vsBaseline") lm.obj <- get.embedding(lm.obj,     .full.model = "tp_contrasts",     .contrast.of.interest = "D7vsBaseline")  # Each embedding is rank 1 with a clear biological meaning: plotSampleEmbedding(lm.obj, .embedding = "pePC",     .sup.embed.slot = "D1vsBaseline", .color.by = "Timepoint") plotSampleEmbedding(lm.obj, .embedding = "pePC",     .sup.embed.slot = "D7vsBaseline", .color.by = "Timepoint") } # }

title: Graph embedding of landmarks — get.graph
text: function orchestrates graph-based analysis following order: 1. k-NN Graph Construction: Builds k-nearest neighbor graph PCA/Harmony embeddings converts multiple representations different downstream applications: adj.matrix: Non-symmetric sparse adjacency matrix (used compute SNN graph) snn: Shared nearest neighbor graph via Jaccard similarity (used clustering) W.sym: Symmetrized binary adjacency (used Laplacian Eigenmap spectral analysis) 2. UMAP Model Training: Trains UMAP transformation landmark cells ret_model=TRUE enable projection query cells later. UMAP model includes fuzzy graph (landmark-landmark probabilistic edges) used optional clustering , projection get.map, generates cell-landmark edge weights essential computing fuzzy density matrices. UMAP model primary output get.graph. 3. Laplacian Eigenmap (LE): Computes spectral embedding solving generalized eigenvalue problem graph Laplacian. Dimensionality automatically selected via elbow detection eigenvalue spectrum. LE embedding currently used warm-start initialization clustering. 4. Clustering (Secondary): Applies Leiden algorithm identify communities visualization interpretation. Uses SNN graph (Jaccard similarity) UMAP fuzzy graph depending .cl.method. Small "straggler" clusters absorbed neighbors. useful exploration, clustering required downstream statistical analysis, unless traditional analysis requested later get.lm.

title: Identify landmarks via leverage score sampling — get.landmarks
text: Two-pass landmark selection algorithm: Pass 1 - Initial sampling: RNA: normalize, log-transform, select top HVGs per sample cytometry: use specified markers Compute sample-specific PCA Calculate leverage scores (sum squared PC loadings per cell) Sample landmarks proportionally leverage scores Pass 2 - Refinement: Pool landmarks samples Compute dataset-wide PCA pooled landmarks Project cells onto shared PC space Recalculate leverage scores using shared PCA Resample landmarks improved scores (final set) two-pass approach ensures landmarks representative global (just sample-specific) variation patterns. Leverage score sampling prioritizes cells high-variance regions maintaining diversity. Optional Harmony integration: .harmony.var specified setup.tdr.obj, performs batch correction landmark PC/SVD embeddings. creates Symphony reference object mapping query cells batch-corrected space. Supported RNA cytometry assay types. cytometry, Harmony corrects batch effects full SVD embedding marker matrix (one dimension per marker). Cytometry data pre-transformed (e.g., arcsinh, logicle) entering tinydenseR pipeline.

title: Differential Density Testing — get.lm
text: landmark-based DA testing workflow: Computes log2(fuzzy density + 0.5) landmark across samples Fits linear model: lmFit(y ~ design) blocking specified: estimates within-block correlation via duplicateCorrelation Applies contrasts provided Performs empirical Bayes moderation: eBayes() Computes density-weighted FDR PCA-weighted q-values Performs traditional cluster/celltype-level DA comparison Advantages cluster-level testing: Detects subtle shifts within clusters arbitrary clustering thresholds Continuous representation cell state space Powered limma's variance shrinkage Design matrix considerations: Include intercept standard comparisons -intercept models continuous covariates assume zero-centering (warning issued) Must full rank (checked automatically) PCA-weighted q-value procedure: Standard FDR methods assume independent tests, landmarks correlated cell state space. account : Residualize PCs: Regress design matrix landmark PCA embeddings obtain covariates independent experimental factors Estimate pi0: Use swfdr::lm_pi0 estimate proportion true nulls conditional PC coordinates. Landmarks similar cell states correlated p-values; PCA captures spatial structure Conservative safeguard: global pi0 < 0.6, apply pi0 floor 0.6 prevent anti-conservative estimates high-signal regimes Compute q-values: Use swfdr::lm_qvalue calculate spatially-weighted FDR properly accounts landmark correlation structure approach powerful standard BH adjustment landmarks exhibit correlated expression, maintaining proper FDR control. Falls back standard q-value BH small test sets (<1000 landmarks).

title: Differential Density Testing — get.lm
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # After mapping lm.obj <- setup.tdr.obj(.cells = .cells, .meta = .meta) |>   get.landmarks() |> get.graph() |> get.map()  # Simple two-group comparison (results stored in lm.obj$map$lm$default) design <- model.matrix(~ Condition, data = .meta) lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = design)  # Visualize results plotBeeswarm(lm.obj, .coefs = "ConditionB")  # Complex design with contrasts design <- model.matrix(~ 0 + Group, data = .meta) contrasts <- limma::makeContrasts(   TvsC = GroupTreatment - GroupControl,   T1vsT2 = GroupTreatment1 - GroupTreatment2,   levels = design ) lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = design, .contrasts = contrasts,                     .model.name = "full", .force.recalc = TRUE)  # With blocking for paired samples design <- model.matrix(~ Timepoint, data = .meta) lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = design, .block = "Subject",                     .model.name = "timepoint")  # Nested model comparison: fit reduced model red.design <- model.matrix(~ 1, data = .meta)  # intercept only lm.obj <- get.lm(lm.obj, .design = red.design, .model.name = "reduced")  # Access results by model name lm.obj$map$lm$full$fit$coefficients lm.obj$map$lm$reduced$fit$coefficients } # }

title: Pseudobulk Differential Expression Analysis — get.pbDE
text: x TDRObj, Seurat, SingleCellExperiment, HDF5AnnData (anndataR) object processed get.map(). ... Additional arguments passed methods. .source raw data object non-file backends. NULL (default) files backend; otherwise Seurat, SingleCellExperiment, anndataR AnnData object. Used .get_sample_matrix() retrieve per-sample expression matrices. .mode Character: analysis mode. One "design" "marker". NULL (default), auto-detected arguments: .design provided \(\Rightarrow\) "design" .id/.id.idx provided without .design \(\Rightarrow\) "marker" .design Design matrix specifying experimental design (design mode ). Rows = samples, columns = coefficients. .contrasts Optional contrast matrix specific comparisons (design mode ). Create limma::makeContrasts(). NULL, tests .design coefficients. .block Optional character: column name .tdr.obj$metadata blocking factor (design mode , e.g., "Donor"). Accounts within-block correlation. .geneset.ls Optional named list character vectors defining gene sets GSVA enrichment analysis. RNA data. Example: list("Tcell" = c("CD3D", "CD3E"), "Bcell" = c("CD19", "MS4A1")). .id Optional character vector cluster/celltype IDs. design mode, restricts analysis cells matching IDs. marker mode, defines group 1 (test group). .id.idx Optional integer vector specifying landmark indices. design mode, restricts analysis cells confidently assigned landmarks. marker mode, defines group 1 landmark indices. .id2 Character vector cluster/celltype IDs group 2 (marker mode ). Default "....landmarks.." compares group 1 cells. Can specify specific IDs pairwise comparisons. .id2.idx Optional integer vector specifying landmark indices group 2 (marker mode ). provided, takes priority .id2. .id.Character: "clustering" "celltyping". Source IDs .id .id2. Default NULL (resolved "clustering" needed). .model.name Character string naming model fit (default "default"). .result.name Character string naming result. design mode defaults ""; marker mode auto-generated .id .id2. Used storage key: $pbDE[[.model.name]][[.result.name]] (design) $markerDE[[.model.name]][[.result.name]] (marker). .population.name NULL. Deprecated alias .result.name (design mode). .comparison.name NULL. Deprecated alias .result.name (marker mode). .force.recalc Logical: TRUE, overwrite existing results specified slot (default FALSE). .verbose Logical: print progress messages? Default TRUE. .label.confidence Numeric scalar [0,1] controlling minimum posterior confidence required assign cell set target landmarks (used .id.idx .id2.idx provided). Default 0.5.

title: Pseudobulk Differential Expression Analysis — get.pbDE
text: Design mode (.mode = "design"): Aggregates cells pseudobulk samples using fuzzy landmark membership, uses limma-voom (RNA) limma (cytometry) test DE across experimental conditions. Uses user-supplied design matrix samples replicates. Marker mode (.mode = "marker"): Identifies marker genes/proteins distinguishing one cell population another (others) via within-sample paired comparison. Unlike design mode tests experimental conditions, marker mode compares cell populations find defining features.

title: Pseudobulk Differential Expression Analysis — get.pbDE
text: Tests DE across experimental conditions using user-supplied design matrix: Cell selection: .id specified, select matching cells. .id.idx specified, use fuzzy confidence thresholding. Otherwise use cells. Pseudobulk aggregation: Fuzzy-weighted expression per sample. Outlier removal: (RNA ) Exclude samples <10\ Normalization: TMM + voom (RNA) -(cytometry). Linear modeling: limma::lmFit optional blocking. Empirical Bayes: limma::eBayes(robust = TRUE). GSVA (optional, RNA ): Gene set variation analysis.

title: Pseudobulk Differential Expression Analysis — get.pbDE
text: Compares cell populations via within-sample paired design (~ .ids + .pairs): Cell selection: Extract cells group 1 (.id) group 2 (.id2). Pseudobulk aggregation: Independent aggregation per group per sample. Outlier removal: (RNA ) Cross-group outlier flagging. Paired comparison: limma::lmFit sample--pair blocking. Positive logFC = higher group 1, negative = higher group 2.

title: Pseudobulk Differential Expression Analysis — get.pbDE
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # After mapping lm.cells <- setup.tdr.obj(.cells = .cells, .meta = .meta) |>   get.landmarks() |> get.graph() |> get.map()  # --- Design mode (default when .design is provided) --- design <- model.matrix(~ Condition, data = .meta) lm.cells <- get.pbDE(lm.cells, .design = design) plotPbDE(lm.cells, .coefs = "ConditionB")  # DE within specific cell type lm.cells <- get.pbDE(lm.cells, .design = design,                      .id = c("1", "2", "3"),                      .id.from = "clustering",                      .result.name = "tcells")  # --- Marker mode (auto-detected when .id is provided without .design) --- lm.cells <- get.pbDE(lm.cells, .mode = "marker",                      .id = "cluster.3",                      .result.name = "cluster3_markers") plotMarkerDE(lm.cells, .comparison.name = "cluster3_markers")  # Pairwise comparison lm.cells <- get.pbDE(lm.cells, .mode = "marker",                      .id = c("cluster.1", "cluster.3"),                      .id2 = c("cluster.2", "cluster.4"),                      .result.name = "cd4_vs_cd8")  # Access results lm.cells$pbDE$default$all$adj.p lm.cells$markerDE$default$cluster3_markers$coefficients } # }

title: Graph-Diffused, Density Contrast-Aligned PLS Decomposition (plsD) — get.plsD
text: Decomposes expression interaction matrix M.local via NIPALS PLS1 density-contrast vector Y. plsD generates candidate features drive density contrast — including population markers (DA), differentially expressed genes (DE), mixture — maximizing covariance graph-smoothed expression Y.

title: Graph-Diffused, Density Contrast-Aligned PLS Decomposition (plsD) — get.plsD
text: x TDRObj, Seurat, SingleCellExperiment, HDF5AnnData (anndataR) object get.lm(). ... Additional arguments passed methods. .coef.col Character: column name model coefficients use density contrast Y. Must valid column .tdr.obj$map$lm[[.model.name]]$fit$coefficients. .model.name Character: name fitted model use (default "default"). .ncomp Integer: number PLS components. Defaults ncol(.tdr.obj$pca$embed), matching number PCs get.landmarks. .min.prop Numeric: RNA, minimum proportion landmarks gene must detected (>0) included. Default 0.005 (0.5 percent). .store.M Logical: TRUE, store M.local output. Default FALSE (saves memory; can large RNA). .degree.reg Logical: TRUE, apply degree regularization adding tau * (self-loops) SNN row-normalizing. Default FALSE. .tau.mult Numeric: multiplier tau .degree.reg = TRUE. tau = .tau.mult * mean(degree). Default 1. .lazy.alpha Numeric (0, 1]: mixing parameter lazy random walk. P_lazy = (1 - alpha) * + alpha * P. Default 1 (standard walk). Values < 1 reduce spatial propagation range expression scores graph, making decomposition local. particularly useful density contrast dominated single biologically extreme population: reducing .lazy.alpha limits far structural score counterweight (see Details) propagates distant manifold regions. Try 0.5 first step strong unexpected balancing observed. Values < 1 also incidentally damp oscillations sparse/irregular graphs. .YX.interaction Logical: TRUE (default), construct M.local = P diag(Y) Xc (Y-weighted interaction). Loadings capture candidate features driving density contrast differential abundance (population markers) differential expression. FALSE, construct M.local = P Xc (graph-smoothed expression ; Y appears response side). FALSE mode less comprehensive — misses DA markers unless expression independently covaries Y. Note: Y centered forming diag(Y), interaction weights landmarks deviation mean coefficient, zero. Landmarks -average (still positive) raw coefficients receive negative weight, contributing structural opposite pole decomposition. .loading.method Character: method computing feature loadings. "pearson" computes Pearson correlation component scores centered expression (scale-invariant, fast sparse-BLAS path). "ols" computes OLS regression coefficients centered Xc component scores (sparse-BLAS path; preserves magnitude information scale-dependent, high-variance features rank higher). "spearman" computes Spearman rank correlation via sparse-aware implementation (robust outliers nonlinearity; slower, O(nnz * log(m_g) * K)). .residualize Logical: TRUE, project nuisance covariates expression matrix decomposition. Default FALSE. Nuisance columns identified automatically: contrasts present, design columns participate contrast nuisance; contrasts used, design columns except .coef.col nuisance. get.lm() called .block, blocking variable additionally included nuisance design (dummy-coded). design expanded sample level landmark level via @config$key. Sparsity expression matrix preserved via implicit operators (residualized matrix never materialized). supported .loading.method = "spearman". .verbose Logical: print progress messages? Default TRUE.

title: Create a hierarchical subset TDRObj — get.subset
text: Given parent TDRObj processed get.map, creates new child TDRObj containing cells matching specified selection criteria. child references parent's expression data without copying: file-backed data, RDS paths reused index filter; -memory backends (Seurat, SCE, matrix, cytoset), index vectors intersected.

title: Create a hierarchical subset TDRObj — get.subset
text: x TDRObj, Seurat, SingleCellExperiment object processed get.map. ... Additional arguments (currently unused). .source raw data object non-file backends (NULL files backend). calling bare TDRObj, pass original Seurat SCE object seurat/sce backends. calling via dispatch wrapper (get.subset.Seurat), filled automatically. .id Optional character vector cluster celltype labels keep. Exactly one .id .id.idx must provided. .id.idx Optional integer vector landmark indices. Cells selected via fuzzy confidence-thresholded voting using stored UMAP fuzzy graphs. .id.Character: "clustering" (default) "celltyping".  Source labels .id used. .label.confidence Numeric scalar [0,1]. Minimum confidence fuzzy label assignment. Defaults parent's stored threshold (@config\$label.confidence), 0.5 set. .prop.landmarks Numeric 0 1 specifying proportion cells use landmarks child pipeline runs get.landmarks().  Default 0.1 (10 percent). .min.cells.per.sample Integer.  Samples fewer qualifying cells threshold excluded child (default 10). .verbose Logical: print progress? Default TRUE.

title: Create a hierarchical subset TDRObj — get.subset
text: new TDRObj : @cells Modified per-sample cell references (expression data copied). @metadata Filtered retained samples, updated n.cells. @config Inherited parameters (backend, assay.type, markers, harmony.var) plus provenance @config\$.subset.provenance. slots Empty – ready get.landmarks().

title: Create a hierarchical subset TDRObj — get.subset
text: child object returned empty analysis slots (assay, landmarks, graphs, density, cellmap, results). user must re-run full pipeline (get.landmarks \(\\) get.graph \(\\) get.map \(\\) get.lm \(\\) ...) child obtain subset-specific results. Cell selection uses machinery get.pbDE: .id: select cells whose per-cell label (@cellmap\$clustering\$ids @cellmap\$celltyping\$ids) matches specified identifiers. .id.idx: select cells via fuzzy confidence-thresholded voting specified landmark indices (using UMAP fuzzy simplicial set stored @cellmap\$fuzzy.graphs). Nested subsetting supported: calling get.subset() child object creates grandchild still references original expression data without additional copies.

title: Create a hierarchical subset TDRObj — get.subset
text: Subset objects session-scoped. Serialization via saveRDS supported child TDRObjs reference -memory backends (seurat, sce, matrix, cyto). files backend, child can saved long parent's RDS files remain disk.

what: Leiden clustering with straggler absorption — leiden.cluster
title: Leiden clustering with straggler absorption — leiden.cluster
text: Performs community detection similarity graph using Leiden algorithm, followed reassignment small "straggler" clusters better-connected neighbors. improves cluster quality preventing tiny, isolated groups.

title: Leiden clustering with straggler absorption — leiden.cluster
code: leiden.cluster(   .tdr.obj = NULL,   .sim.matrix,   .resolution.parameter,   .small.size = floor((nrow(x = .sim.matrix)/200)),   .verbose = TRUE,   .seed = 123 )

title: Leiden clustering with straggler absorption — leiden.cluster
text: .tdr.obj Optional tinydenseR object get.landmarks() get.graph(). provided, uses Laplacian Eigenmap embedding initialization (computed successfully), otherwise falls back PCA embedding. NULL, computes 3D embedding .sim.matrix via truncated SVD. .sim.matrix Square similarity matrix (typically Jaccard similarity k-NN graphs). Higher values indicate stronger connections nodes. Self-loops (diagonal) automatically removed. .resolution.parameter Numeric controlling cluster granularity Leiden CPM algorithm. Higher values (e.g., 0.0005-0.005) yield /smaller clusters. Lower values yield fewer/larger clusters. Optimal value depends data scale. .small.size Integer threshold straggler clusters. Clusters fewer cells absorbed neighbors. Default: 0.5% total nodes (floor(n/200)). .verbose Logical, print progress cluster counts. Default TRUE. .seed Integer reproducibility (affects k-means Leiden). Default 123.

title: Leiden clustering with straggler absorption — leiden.cluster
text: Factor vector cluster assignments levels ordered appearance. Labels formatted "cluster.01", "cluster.02", etc.

title: Leiden clustering with straggler absorption — leiden.cluster
text: Algorithm steps: Initialize k-means (25 centers) embedding avoid singletons Run Leiden community detection CPM objective function Identify "straggler" clusters smaller .small.size straggler, compute mean connectivity keeper clusters Reassign straggler cells connected keeper cluster Relabel clusters sequentially Initialization strategy: Uses Laplacian Eigenmap embedding (computed available .tdr.obj$graph$LE$embed), otherwise PCA embedding (first 3 components), computes 3D embedding similarity matrix via truncated SVD (irlba) .tdr.obj NULL. K-means initialization helps Leiden start reasonable communities rather singleton nodes, improving stability. Straggler absorption: Small clusters (< 0.5% total default) often represent noise transitional states. Rather keeping separate clusters, merged similar neighboring cluster based mean edge weights. sparse similarity matrices, mean computed normalizing sum stored values full submatrix size properly account implicit zeros.

Additional arguments passed methods. .model.name Character string naming model fit use .tdr.obj$map$lm (default "default"). title: Bee Swarm Plot of Density Estimate Change — plotBeeswarm
text: beeswarm layout prevents overlapping points, making easier assess distribution effect sizes across landmarks. title: Plot plsD Scores (Diagnostic and Component Views) — plotPlsD
text: Component view (.plsD.dim = integer): Left panel: UMAP (PCA) embedding colored PLS scores (diverging scale) Right panel: Scatter centered Y vs PLS scores, colored raw (uncentered) density contrast coefficient — essential distinguishing genuine contrast signal structural score balancing Right panel scatter note: landmarks colored raw (uncentered) density contrast coefficient (centered Y x-axis). intentional: cluster landmarks negative scores shows warm raw-Y colors (near zero positive raw coefficient), likely structural geometric counterweight rather genuinely depleted population. reasoning applies reverse: large-magnitude positive-score landmarks near-zero raw Y may reflect structural balance opposite side, depending contrast direction. Diagnostic view (.plsD.dim = NULL): X-axis: Smoothness (Sk); higher = large-scale graph-smooth structure Y-axis: Y-alignment (Ak); higher = stronger density coupling Color: |q_k| (Y-loading magnitude; larger = Y variance captured) Labels: Component indices (1, 2, ...) Warning: high Ak + high Sk (top-left region diagnostic plot) typical pattern component dominated single extreme population. Inspect corresponding component view score-vs-Y scatter interpreting gene loadings. Ak high construction early components: NIPALS PLS1 maximizes covariance Y every deflation step. diagnostic scatter useful identifying components also graph-smooth (high Sk).

text: Feature selection ordering: Features selected absolute regression loading first element .plsD.dim. RNA, top .n.features/2 positive top .n.features/2 negative-loaded features selected. Selected features ordered signed loading (highest positive top, negative bottom). Loadings: Uses OLS regression loadings (Xc ~ score_k): loading slope per-gene regression component score, capturing marker-component association. Positive loadings = genes upregulated high-score (high-Y) landmarks; negative loadings = genes upregulated low-score landmarks. datasets structural score balancing (see get.plsD Details), large-magnitude loadings either end ranking may partially reflect geometric mean-zero constraint rather genuine differential expression. Use raw Y annotation strip plotPlsD scatter assess whether extreme-loading features correspond landmarks genuine density contrast signal. Caution: depending direction contrast component, large-magnitude features either positive negative end loading ranking may reflect structural score balancing (geometric mean-zero constraint) rather genuine biology. Always cross-reference raw Y annotation strip: feature large negative loading highly expressed landmarks near-zero raw Y likely characterizing structural counterweight region. Expression normalization (RNA ): Computes row sums (per-landmark library size) full raw.landmarks Subsets top features Applies size factor normalization Log2-transforms: log2(x + 1) Centers feature (row) mean 0 Annotation strips: Density contrast: raw (uncentered) log2 fold change linear model. Zero = density change. Positive = enriched contrast direction; negative = depleted. plsD scores dimensions .plsD.dim Custom annotations (.order.matrix columns, .add.annot columns)
